context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the SWOT analysis for my city case study in the climate action course. I’m a little stuck on how to turn my earlier work, like the spider diagram and actor map, into actual strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. Could you help me start with that?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but you need to do the sorting. Start by asking: which findings are inside the city’s current control, and which come from outside conditions?

Use this test:
- Internal + helpful = strength
- Internal + limiting = weakness
- External + helpful = opportunity
- External + harmful = threat

Now pick just 1 pathway. From your spider diagram, which pathway scored weakest or needs the most work? And from your actor map, which actor pattern in that pathway helps action, and which pattern blocks it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role, keep the response brief, and use Socratic questioning to move the student from confusion toward a workable method without supplying content for their SWOT. I will connect directly to the assignment: spider diagram, power/actor map, and stressors. I will not write any SWOT entries for them; instead I will give a minimal decision framework and ask targeted questions that help them generate their own examples.

exchanges.student: I think one of the weakest pathways in my city is governance and civic engagement, but I’m not totally sure if I’m labeling that right. On the actor map, I think the helpful pattern is that the city government and some NGOs are already involved, and the blocking pattern is that community groups don’t seem to have much power. Is that the right kind of answer?
exchanges.tutor:
That is the right kind of evidence, yes. But right now it’s still descriptive, not yet a SWOT item.

Test each point:
- “City government and NGOs are already involved” — is that an internal advantage your city already has?
- “Community groups have little power” — is that an internal limitation in decision-making?

If so, what category would each one fall into? And what concrete proof do you have for each—policy, program, participation process, budget, coalition, or missing representation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm what is valid, correct imprecision, and push the student to classify their observations into SWOT categories themselves. I will keep it concise, ask forward-moving questions, and avoid drafting the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think “city government and NGOs are already involved” would be a strength because it’s internal and helpful. And “community groups have little power” sounds like a weakness because it’s internal and limiting. I’m getting stuck on the proof part though—do I need formal data for every item, or can I use things like a climate plan, advisory committee structure, or public meeting process?
exchanges.tutor:
You do not need formal data for every item. The assignment allows policies, initiatives, and structures as evidence.

Stronger support usually comes from things like:
- a climate plan or ordinance
- advisory committee design
- public participation process
- budget/program commitments
- attendance or representation patterns, if available

Now choose one of your two draft items. What is one specific document or process from your city that could serve as evidence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will answer the process question without giving content, anchor to the assignment requirement for specific examples and relevant data/policies/initiatives where possible, and prompt the student to identify evidence types for their own city. This keeps guidance minimal and solution-oriented.

exchanges.student: I can probably use my city’s climate action plan as evidence for the strength item, since it shows the city government is already engaged. For the weakness, I might use the public participation process if it shows outreach happened but decision-making still stayed concentrated. Does that make sense, or is that still too vague?
exchanges.tutor:
It makes sense, but yes—still too vague. “Climate plan exists” alone is weak unless you show what in it demonstrates capacity or coordination.

Ask yourself:
- What feature of the plan shows real strength: targets, funding, interagency coordination, implementation office, reporting?
- What feature of participation shows weakness: low representation, consultation without influence, no community leadership, uneven outreach?

Pick one feature for each. What exactly did you observe?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the direction, point out that the student needs more specificity, and ask narrowing questions that move them toward concrete, assignment-ready entries without writing them. I will remain brief and focused on the SWOT task.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think for the strength I should point to something more specific like an implementation framework or interagency coordination in the climate plan, not just the plan existing. For the weakness, I think what I observed was more like consultation without much influence, where residents could comment but weren’t really part of decision-making. I’m not sure how to phrase those as short SWOT entries though.
exchanges.tutor:
You need to write the entries yourself—I won’t phrase them for you. But use a simple formula:

- Noun/feature + why it helps or limits climate action
- Then attach 1 concrete piece of evidence

So for each item, fill in:
1. What city feature am I naming?
2. Why is it helpful/limiting for this pathway?
3. What evidence proves it?

Try drafting just one strength in your own words, in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for phrasing, but the rules prohibit writing or suggesting language for them. I will maintain that boundary, explain briefly, and use guided prompts to help them generate their own concise entries. This preserves the tutor role and keeps the student doing the writing.

exchanges.student: Okay, my draft is: the city’s interagency climate coordination is a strength because it helps align departments around implementation, and I’d use the climate action plan’s assigned responsibilities or reporting structure as evidence. Is that close enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—close enough to be usable. The logic is sound. What still needs work is specificity.

Push it one step further: which departments or what reporting mechanism shows that coordination is real rather than symbolic?

If you can name that concrete feature, your entry becomes much stronger. Then do the same for your weakness.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give concise evaluative feedback without grading, note what is strong, identify what remains weak, and ask one next-step question that advances the student's own revision. I will not rewrite the sentence.

exchanges.student: I think I need to go back and check which departments were actually named, because right now I only remember that responsibilities were divided across agencies. For the weakness, I’d say limited community influence is a weakness because residents were consulted through meetings or comments, but they didn’t seem to share decision-making power. Can you help me tell whether that weakness is specific enough yet?
exchanges.tutor:
It’s conceptually solid, but still one step too general. You’ve identified the weakness; now you need proof of limited influence, not just participation.

Ask:
- What shows residents lacked power rather than just access?
- Were they advisory only?
- Were decisions already set before consultation?
- Was there no shared governance body or no community vote/oversight role?

Which of those fits your city best?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will assess the student's weakness entry briefly, indicate what criterion it still lacks, and ask a targeted question to help them find evidence. This maintains guided discovery and avoids composing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I think the best fit is that residents were mostly advisory only, and I don’t think there was a shared governance body with actual decision power. So the weakness would be about advisory participation without shared authority. I’m still a little unsure how to distinguish that from a threat, though.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re close. The distinction is about where the problem comes from.

- Weakness: an internal design problem in the city’s current process
- Threat: an external condition that could worsen or exploit that problem

So ask yourself: is “advisory-only participation” something built into how the city currently governs? If yes, what does that make it?

Then what outside force could threaten progress in this pathway—political turnover, distrust, budget cuts, misinformation, competing crises?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will address the conceptual confusion directly but still through a concise distinction and a check-for-understanding question. This moves toward the solution without giving the student's content outright.

exchanges.student: Yeah, if advisory-only participation is built into the city’s process, then that makes it a weakness, not a threat. A threat would be something outside that could make it worse, like political turnover or budget cuts reducing engagement even more. I think I’m starting to get it—could you check if that distinction sounds right before I move on?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that distinction is right. You’ve separated internal process flaws from external risks correctly.

Now don’t linger here too long. Apply the same test to one opportunity for this pathway:
- external
- helpful
- feasible given your actor map
- responsive to a weakness or stressor

What’s one candidate opportunity in your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's understanding, reinforce the core distinction, and direct them to the next productive step in the assignment. I will keep it brief, avoid providing content, and ask them to apply the framework to another quadrant or pathway so they continue doing the work.

exchanges.student: I think one opportunity could be external funding or a partnership with universities or NGOs to strengthen community engagement. That seems external and helpful, and maybe feasible if those actors already show up in my actor map. I’m not fully sure how to make it specific enough yet.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right direction, but “funding or partnership” is too broad. Choose one.

Test it with 3 questions:
- What exact external source or partner do you mean?
- How would it specifically improve civic engagement, not just climate work generally?
- What in your actor map makes it feasible?

Pick one opportunity and answer those 3 in note form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's conceptual progress, prevent vagueness by pushing for one concrete opportunity, and tie it back to assignment criteria: feasibility, actor map, and evidence. I will not draft the SWOT entry or provide language, only ask targeted questions that help the student specify it.
